title: Pikelets
subtitle: Thin yeasted griddle cakes with a holed surface
excerpt: Pikelets are a thinner, lacier cousin of the crumpet: a yeasted flour batter cooked in a dry frying pan until the surface is covered in holes and the underside is pale gold. Eat hot with butter. Makes twelve.
sourceNotes: Pikelets are documented in British domestic cookery from the eighteenth century onward, particularly in the north of England and the Midlands. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: pikelets are recorded as a thin yeasted griddle cake cooked without a crumpet ring, producing a flatter, lacier result. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: a thin flour-and-milk batter set with yeast and cooked on a hot iron is documented as a regional variant of the crumpet.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked pikelets in a sealed bag for up to 1 month. Toast from frozen.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: medium heat throughout; the pan is ready when a drop of water skitters immediately across the surface

content.text: The batter should be pourable but not watery — thicker than crêpe batter, thinner than pancake batter. The holes form as carbon dioxide from the yeast bubbles through the surface while the pikelet cooks; do not flip until the holes have mostly set and the surface looks nearly dry. A heavy non-stick pan holds heat more evenly than a thin one.

Whisk the {{plain-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} together in a large bowl. Make a well, pour in the warm {{whole-milk}}, and whisk to a smooth batter. Cover with a clean tea towel and leave at room temperature for 45 minutes until the surface is bubbly.

Stir the {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} into the proved batter. Heat the pan over medium heat without any oil or butter. When a drop of water skitters across the surface, ladle about 3 tablespoons of batter per pikelet into the pan; cook 2 or 3 at a time without crowding them. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes until the surface is covered in holes and looks nearly dry. Flip and cook for 1 minute more until just set on the second side. Keep warm on a plate covered with a tea towel while you cook the rest. Eat with butter while still warm.
